message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 40
Recent Green phase: Added a guard at the top of damageReimbursement that throws Error("Damage amount must be non-negative: ...") when damage.amount < 0.

There are now several validation guards in the claim path: requireDamagesWithinCover (in claim), requireInsuredItem (in damageReimbursement), and the new negative-amount check (in damageReimbursement). Evaluate whether the claim-time validations should be consolidated for clarity (e.g. a single validateClaim or a set of small named guards called together at claim entry), versus being scattered. Keep behavior identical and all tests green. Run tests with `pnpm test`. Do not add behavior for future tests (only the unknown-type-in-quote error test remains).

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Now let me run the tests to confirm the current green state before refactoring.

content.text:
All 40 tests pass. Now let me analyze the refactoring opportunity highlighted in the prompt: the scattered claim-time validations.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

Current validation guards:
- `requireDamagesWithinCover(incident, policy)` — called in `claim`
- `requireInsuredItem(damage, policy)` — called inside `damageReimbursement`
- The negative-amount check — inline in `damageReimbursement`

**Naming assessment:**
- `requireDamagesWithinCover` — clear, reveals intent
- `requireInsuredItem` — clear, but it does double duty: it both *validates* (throws if uninsured) and *returns* the item used for rate computation. Its name leans toward the lookup-with-validation role, which is its actual purpose.
- The negative-amount check is anonymous (inline). It has no name revealing the intent "amounts must be non-negative."

The key question: should these be consolidated into a single claim-entry validation, or kept where they are?

## Step 2: Analyze the structure

The three guards differ in their natural location:

- `requireDamagesWithinCover` — operates on the **whole incident** (cross-damage counting). Belongs at claim entry.
- The negative-amount check — operates per-damage, but is a **pure precondition** on the input (no policy data needed). It's a validation concern, not a computation concern.
- `requireInsuredItem` — operates per-damage, but it is **load-bearing for the computation**: it returns the `Item` whose `enchantment` drives the reimbursement rate. It is not purely a guard; inlining it elsewhere would require a second lookup.

So the negative-amount check is the misfit: it sits inside the *computation* function (`damageReimbursement`) but is purely a *precondition*. Mixing a precondition into the reimbursement math hurts Rule 2 (reveals intent) — `damageReimbursement` should compute a reimbursement, not police input.

## Step 3: APP Mass — current `damageReimbursement`

```
const damageReimbursement = (damage, policy) => {
  if (damage.amount < 0) {                                    // conditional 4; bindings damage.amount=1, 0=1
    throw new Error(`...: ${damage.amount}`);                 // invocation 2; binding 1; constant(template) 1
  }
  const item = requireInsuredItem(damage, policy);            // assign 6; invoke 2; bindings damage,policy=2
  const rate = isHighlyEnchanted(item) ? RATE1 : RATE2;       // assign 6; invoke 2; conditional 4; bindings item,RATE1,RATE2=3
  const coveredAmount = rate * damage.amount;                 // assign 6; invoke(*) 2; bindings rate,damage.amount=2
  return coveredAmount - DEDUCTIBLE;                          // invoke(-) 2; bindings coveredAmount,DEDUCTIBLE=2
}
```
Mass of the guard block alone: conditional(4) + amount binding(1) + literal 0(1) + throw invocation(2) + template binding(1) + template constant(1) = **10**.

The plan: extract the negative-amount precondition into a named guard `requireNonNegativeDamages(incident)` and call it at claim entry, next to `requireDamagesWithinCover`. This consolidates the two *pure preconditions* together at claim entry, names the previously-anonymous check, and leaves `requireInsuredItem` where it is (it's load-bearing for the rate computation, not a pure guard).

## Step 4: Implement the refactoring

Extract the negative-amount precondition to a named guard at claim entry.

content.text:
All 40 tests still pass. The negative-amount test in the suite (`damage entry with amount -200 → throws`) goes through `claim`, so the moved guard still triggers it.

## Step 5: APP Mass — after refactoring

**`damageReimbursement` (guard removed):** lost the conditional(4) + amount binding(1) + literal 0(1) + throw invocation(2) + template binding(1) + template constant(1) = **−10 mass** from the computation function.

**New `requireNonNegativeDamages`:** loop(5) + iteration bindings + conditional(4) + amount binding(1) + literal 0(1) + throw invocation(2) + template(1+1) ≈ **15 mass** (the loop adds 5 over the previous inline form, the cost of making it a standalone, named, claim-entry guard).

Net global mass change is roughly +5 (a loop replaced a per-call conditional). Per the rules, **Rule 2 (reveals intent) trumps APP**: the small mass increase buys a named precondition and a computation function that only computes.

## Step 6: Decision

**Refactoring applied — extract claim-entry precondition:**

- **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) — primary win:** The previously anonymous inline `if (damage.amount < 0)` is now a named guard, `requireNonNegativeDamages`, sitting at claim entry directly beside `requireDamagesWithinCover`. The two *pure preconditions* now read as a coherent validation block at the top of `claim`. `damageReimbursement` is now purely a computation (lookup rate, apply, subtract deductible) — it no longer polices its input.
- **Consolidation decision:** I deliberately did **not** fold `requireInsuredItem` into a single mega-validator. It is load-bearing — it returns the `Item` whose `enchantment` the reimbursement rate depends on. Inlining it into a claim-entry validator would force a second lookup of the same item inside `damageReimbursement`, adding duplication (Rule 3 violation) and obscuring why the lookup happens. Keeping it where the returned value is consumed is clearer. So the right consolidation was the two *pure* preconditions, not all three guards.
- **Behavior:** Identical. All 40 tests green; the `-200` test still throws via `claim`.
- **APP:** `damageReimbursement` shed ~10 mass; new guard ~15 mass (a loop replacing a per-element conditional). Net ~+5, accepted because Rule 2 outranks APP.
- **Did not touch** the remaining `it.todo` (unknown-type-in-quote error) — no future behavior added.

## Step 7: Report

```
Refactoring Complete:
Refactoring: Extracted the inline negative-amount check out of damageReimbursement
             into a named guard requireNonNegativeDamages, called at claim entry
             beside requireDamagesWithinCover. Pure preconditions now grouped;
             damageReimbursement now only computes. Left requireInsuredItem in place
             (its return value is load-bearing for the rate).
Mass Change: damageReimbursement −10; new guard +15 (net ~+5, accepted — Rule 2 > APP)
Tests: All 40 passing
```

Implementation file: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_18-30-07_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.2-with-why-cleaned_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`
